title: Simulating social change
text: Building simulation requires three steps: Define population Define outcome function variable interest Define population dynamics (coming age, mortality, - -migration) Step 1: first step define initial population. example, define simple population aged 20 39, uniform distribution age 30, declining age 39. dataset define needs least two columns: age n. can add columns want describe complicated populations, ’ll stick simple setup now. ’ll call row dataset population cell. extreme case, can also define population individuals n just 1. ’s simple population: Step 2: Next, define variable interest want simulate. , define function takes arguments population cells time continuous variable. Time really needs continuous - need know outcome every individual population terms discrete ‘years’ (whatever time scale), every point -well. example, use outcome dependent age: age 20, outcome 0, age 40 outcome 1, values linearly increasing age. purely function age, time, real-world survey, outcome “support investing longevity research” “support increasing pensions” - ’ll just use latter example: defined population terms one variable (age), members age also identical outcome value. Step 3: next step define population dynamics. requires defining four functions: fun_mortality(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_outmigration(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_coming_of_age(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n fun_inmigration(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n functions defined period--period. practice, often years. instance, mortality function return counts define many members population die within next period, population cell. -migration function defined way, just number people migrating population cell. coming age function -migration functions different: return data frame describes new population members added period--period. functions flexible enough describe complicated real-world population dynamics. first simple example, define functions way keeps population stable: don’t migration, mortality coming--age balances perfectly - every period 100 new members age 20 added, 100 members age 30 die: ’re now ready start simulation: simulation result displays overview period, well summary total decomposition change. results year show indeed population stable: end every period population 1550, stable mean 0.38 - .e, 38% population supports increasing pensions. However, stability bit deceptive, everyone’s opinion actually keeps constantly changing. can see looking “Intraindividual change” component, +0.25. Roughly speaking, without population turnover, expected mean support increasing pensions increased quite bit, 63%. makes sense, ’ve designed simulation way support increases age. turn, younger people much less likely support increasing pensions. Hence, enter population, effect decreasing mean. true mortality. Hence, population turnover component negative. simulation, two components balance exactly, leading expected complete stabilty year--year mean.
code: library("data.table")  data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39),     n = c(rep(100, 10), seq.int(100, 10, by = -10)) )  data #>       age     n #>     <int> <num> #>  1:    20   100 #>  2:    21   100 #>  3:    22   100 #>  4:    23   100 #>  5:    24   100 #>  6:    25   100 #>  7:    26   100 #>  8:    27   100 #>  9:    28   100 #> 10:    29   100 #> 11:    30   100 #> 12:    31    90 #> 13:    32    80 #> 14:    33    70 #> 15:    34    60 #> 16:    35    50 #> 17:    36    40 #> 18:    37    30 #> 19:    38    20 #> 20:    39    10 #>       age     n #>     <int> <num> support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, (age - 20) * 1 / 20] } # e.g., at age 20,   support_for_pensions is 0 #       at age 20.5, support_for_pensions is 0.026 #       at age 40,   support_for_pensions is 1 mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 10, 0)] } outmigration <- function(data, period) {     0 } coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = 20, n = 100) } inmigration <- function(data, period) {     data.table(n = 0) } library(socialchange)  simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_mortality = mortality,     fun_outmigration = outmigration,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_inmigration = inmigration ) simresult #>   period   mean    N intraindividual mortality outmigration coming_of_age #>  initial 0.3758 1550              NA        NA           NA            NA #>        1 0.3758 1550            0.05  -0.02412            0      -0.02588 #>        2 0.3758 1550            0.05  -0.02415            0      -0.02585 #>        3 0.3758 1550            0.05  -0.02415            0      -0.02585 #>        4 0.3758 1550            0.05  -0.02417            0      -0.02583 #>        5 0.3758 1550            0.05  -0.02415            0      -0.02585 #>  inmigration #>           NA #>            0 #>            0 #>            0 #>            0 #>            0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial                0.3758 #>  At end                    0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Out-migration         0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #>    - In-migration          0.0000

title: Simulating social change
text: slightly complex setup, can add covariates simulation. instance, can introduce gender covariate. example, split former population two equally-distributed genders: also adjust population dynamics functions keep population stable. Note don’t depend gender (): reran simulation settings, get exact results . introduce slight variation, though, determine one gender actually fixed opinion support increasing pensions. gender behaves : Although overall mean doesn’t change time, decomposition results changed. men now contribute population turnover intraindividual change, women , components halved.
code: data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     gender = c(rep("f", 20), rep("m", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5)),         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5))     ) ) mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 5, 0)] } coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), gender = c("f", "m"), n = c(50, 50)) } support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(gender == "f", 0.3758, (age - 20) * 1 / 20)] } simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_mortality = mortality,     fun_outmigration = outmigration,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_inmigration = inmigration ) simresult #>   period   mean    N intraindividual mortality outmigration coming_of_age #>  initial 0.3758 1550              NA        NA           NA            NA #>        1 0.3758 1550         0.02490  -0.01203            0      -0.01287 #>        2 0.3758 1550         0.02495  -0.01203            0      -0.01291 #>        3 0.3758 1550         0.02496  -0.01207            0      -0.01289 #>        4 0.3758 1550         0.02507  -0.01213            0      -0.01294 #>        5 0.3758 1550         0.02493  -0.01203            0      -0.01290 #>  inmigration #>           NA #>            0 #>            0 #>            0 #>            0 #>            0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.37580 #>  At end                    0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.12480 #>  - Population turnover    -0.12480 #>    - Mortality            -0.06029 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06451 #>    - In-migration          0.00000

title: What the Package Does (Title Case)
text: basic example, look decomposition EU membership, whether population growth EU due intracountry changes population, due admission new countries. decomposition unit country. run event_decomposition decomposition function, need two datasets: One dataset contains events, case dates countries entered exited EU. dataset included socialchange package: second dataset contains outcome want decompose point time change events, plus start end period interest. EU formed 1952, countries entered exited 1973, 1981, 1986, 1995, 2004, 2007, 2013, 2020. Countries, course, entered specific dates, population data provided yearly resolutions, assume error introduced using yearly resolution small. socialchange package also includes total population data countries world 1950 onwards, also includes required data: run event_decomposition function, need ensure columns unit time parameters named , prepare data follows: Finally, run decomposition. specify two datasets, formula form Outcome ~ Unit + Time. also need specify end data, aggregation function. Often mean, case needs sum: outcome decomposition tells us intracountry changes population account 30% total population increase (80 million), additions one (Br)exit account 70% total population increase (184 million). also possible produce figure shows decomposition:
code: library(socialchange) socialchange::eu_membership #>           country       date event_type #> 1         Belgium 1952-07-23    initial #> 2          France 1952-07-23    initial #> 3         Germany 1952-07-23    initial #> 4           Italy 1952-07-23    initial #> 5      Luxembourg 1952-07-23    initial #> 6     Netherlands 1952-07-23    initial #> 7  United Kingdom 1973-01-01      entry #> 8         Denmark 1973-01-01      entry #> 9         Ireland 1973-01-01      entry #> 10         Greece 1981-01-01      entry #> 11       Portugal 1986-01-01      entry #> 12          Spain 1986-01-01      entry #> 13        Austria 1995-01-01      entry #> 14         Sweden 1995-01-01      entry #> 15        Finland 1995-01-01      entry #> 16         Cyprus 2004-05-01      entry #> 17          Malta 2004-05-01      entry #> 18        Hungary 2004-05-01      entry #> 19         Poland 2004-05-01      entry #> 20       Slovakia 2004-05-01      entry #> 21         Latvia 2004-05-01      entry #> 22        Estonia 2004-05-01      entry #> 23      Lithuania 2004-05-01      entry #> 24        Czechia 2004-05-01      entry #> 25       Slovenia 2004-05-01      entry #> 26       Bulgaria 2007-01-01      entry #> 27        Romania 2007-01-01      entry #> 28        Croatia 2013-07-01      entry #> 29 United Kingdom 2020-01-31       exit library(data.table) wpp_data <- as.data.table(socialchange::wpp_data) # population data is in 1,000 wpp_data[Location == "Belgium" & Time %in% c(1952, 1973, 1981, 1986, 1995, 2004, 2007, 2013, 2020)] #>    Location  Time  PopTotal #>      <char> <num>     <num> #> 1:  Belgium  1952  8695.582 #> 2:  Belgium  1973  9727.980 #> 3:  Belgium  1981  9843.592 #> 4:  Belgium  1986  9887.815 #> 5:  Belgium  1995 10095.198 #> 6:  Belgium  2004 10456.163 #> 7:  Belgium  2007 10653.697 #> 8:  Belgium  2013 11103.257 #> 9:  Belgium  2020 11561.717 # rename columns setnames(wpp_data, c("Location", "Time"), c("country", "year")) # population in millions wpp_data[, PopTotal := PopTotal / 1000]  # coarsen to year - required as we don't have daily population data eu_membership <- as.data.table(socialchange::eu_membership) eu_membership[, year := year(as.Date(date))] ev <- event_decomposition(eu_membership, wpp_data, PopTotal ~ country + year,   end_period = 2021, fun = sum) print(ev) #>     event_type      term    total      pct #>         <char>     <num>    <num>    <num> #> 1:      change  80.74284 265.3265 30.43151 #> 2: replacement 184.58363 265.3265 69.56849 plot(ev) #> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. #> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. #> ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the socialchange package. #>   Please report the issue to the authors. #> This warning is displayed once per session. #> Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was generated.
